anchor.snippet: a real interval is the set of all real numbers lying between two fixed endpoints
the set of real numbers consisting of 0 , 1 , and all numbers in between is an interval
An interval is a subset of the real numbers that contains all real numbers lying between any two numbers of the subset
The endpoints of an interval are its supremum (least upper bound), and its infimum (greatest lower bound)
Non-empty intervals are completely determined by their endpoints and whether each endpoint belongs to the interval
An open interval does not include any endpoint and can be succinctly indicated with parentheses

annotations.label: Open intervals as base of open sets

anchor.snippet: The open intervals are those intervals that are open sets for the usual topology on the real numbers, and they form a base of the open sets
A closed interval is an interval that includes all its finite endpoints
The closed intervals are those intervals that are closed sets for the usual topology on the real numbers
A half-open interval has two distinct finite endpoints, and includes one but not the other
a set of the real numbers is an interval, if and only if it is an open interval, a closed interval, or a half-open interval
A degenerate interval is any set consisting of a single real number
A real interval that is neither empty nor degenerate is said to be proper
An interval is said to be left-bounded or right-bounded , if there is some real number that is, respectively, smaller than or larger than all its elements
Bounded intervals are bounded sets , in the sense that their diameter (which is equal to the absolute difference between the endpoints) is finite
The centre ( midpoint ) of a bounded interval with endpoints a and b is ( a + b )/2 , and its radius is the half-length

An interval I is a subinterval of interval J if I is a subset of J
The interior of an interval I is the largest open interval that is contained in I
the interval enclosure or interval span of X is the unique interval that contains X

The closure of an interval is the union of the interval and the set of its finite endpoints, and hence is also an interval
The intersection of any collection of intervals is always an interval
